planting.note: Direct sow in fall to allow cold stratification through winter; seeds will germinate in spring as soils warm.

directions: Sunflower-leaved Coreopsis is a robust southeastern native perennial distinguished by its broad, undivided leaves that closely resemble small sunflower leaves — quite unlike the finely cut foliage of most other Coreopsis. Plant in average to moist, well-drained soil in full sun; it tolerates a wider range of conditions than many of its relatives and is more forgiving of heavier soils. Classic bright yellow daisy flowers with maroon-tinged central discs appear on upright stems from June through August, reliably attracting bees and butterflies. The plant spreads slowly by rhizomes to form attractive, long-lived clumps that require little maintenance in prairie gardens or open sunny borders.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center Native Plant Database

planting.note: Direct sow in fall; plants establish a basal rosette the first year and flower in late spring of the second year.

directions: Carolina Woollywhite is a native biennial or short-lived perennial in the aster family, native to dry, sandy, open ground, pine barrens, and roadsides from the Carolinas to Florida and Texas. In its first year it forms a basal rosette of deeply pinnate, fern-like leaves covered in soft white woolly hair; in its second year it sends up branched, upright stems 2 to 4 feet tall topped with open, corymb-like clusters of small white to cream composite flowers in late spring. Grow in dry, lean, sandy, acidic soil in full sun — rich or heavy soils will cause it to decline; it is quite drought-tolerant once established. Allow plants to set seed freely, as self-sowing is the primary means of maintaining a colony; the small seeds are a food source for sparrows and finches in native garden plantings.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center Native Plant Database

planting.note: Direct sow in fall into dry, lean soil; plants spend the first year as a basal rosette and flower in their second year.

directions: Old Plainsman is a tall, strikingly elegant native biennial or short-lived perennial found on dry, open prairies, mesquite grasslands, and roadsides primarily in Texas and adjacent south-central states. In its first year it produces an aromatic, white-woolly basal rosette of deeply dissected, fern-like foliage; in its second year it erects much-branched stems to 5 feet tall, topped with flat, dense clusters of small creamy-white to yellowish composite flowers from May through July that are highly attractive to native bees and small butterflies. Grow in dry, calcareous or sandy, well-drained, lean soil in full sun — it will not tolerate heavy, wet, or highly fertile soils. Allow plants to self-sow to maintain a continuing colony; the airy, open texture and tall stature make it an outstanding component of Texas Hill Country and western prairie native gardens.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center Native Plant Database
Wildflower Center Digital Library — Texas Native Plants

planting.note: Direct sow on or just after the last frost date; seeds germinate readily in warm sandy soil and plants grow quickly to bloom.

directions: Small Palafox is a slender, branching native annual of the aster family, found naturally in dry, sandy grasslands, prairies, and open roadsides across Texas and adjacent south-central states. It produces clusters of small, rose-pink to pale lavender composite flowers with a deeper rose disc from midsummer through fall, providing reliable late-season color in hot, dry gardens. Sow seeds directly in spring after the last frost into dry, sandy, lean, well-drained soil in full sun; plants thrive on neglect and perform best without supplemental fertilizer or irrigation. Small Palafox self-sows freely in open, disturbed sandy ground, making it a reliable naturalizer and low-maintenance filler for Texas native wildflower plantings and sandy prairie restorations.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center Native Plant Database
Weakley's Flora of the Southeastern United States

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in dry, well-drained calcareous or sandy soil; established plants are long-lived and persistent with minimal care.

directions: Reverchon's Palafox is a Texas-endemic native perennial, restricted in the wild to dry, sandy, or caliche-rocky soils across central and west Texas prairies and plains. Unlike the annual Small Palafox, this species forms woody-based, mounding clumps with narrow, alternately arranged leaves on branching stems that persist from year to year and expand slowly over time. Clusters of rose-pink composite flowers appear from late summer through fall, reliably drawing monarch butterflies and native bees at a critical time in the migration season. Grow in full sun in dry, lean, calcareous or sandy, sharply drained soil and provide little to no supplemental irrigation once established; it is an outstanding drought-tolerant native perennial for Texas xeriscapes, caliche gardens, and rocky prairie plantings.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center Native Plant Database
Texas Endemic Plants — Native Plant Society of Texas
